title: The Person and Work of Christ, Part 4
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 5-5-24 - Person and Work of Christ - Part 4 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Review and Setup: Westminster Larger Catechism Q. 42-43
subpoints: Q. 42: Why was our mediator called Christ? Because He was anointed with the Holy Ghost above measure, set apart and fully furnished to execute the offices of prophet, priest, and king
Q. 43: How does Christ execute the office of a prophet? By revealing to the church in all ages by His spirit and word the whole will of God concerning their edification and salvation
Two questions to answer: Were prophets, priests, and kings anointed into their offices? Was Christ anointed into these offices?

outline.title: Prophets, Priests, and Kings Were Anointed into Their Offices
subpoints: Priests: Exodus 28 — Aaron and his sons were anointed and sanctified to minister as priests
Kings: 1 Samuel 16 — God directed Samuel to anoint David as king over Israel
Prophets: Psalm 105:13-15 — God's 'anointed ones' and His 'prophets' are placed in parallel (synonymous), showing prophets were considered anointed ones
1 Kings 19:16 — God commanded Elijah to anoint Elisha as prophet in his place
Conclusion: Prophets, priests, and kings in Israel were generally initiated into their offices by anointing

outline.title: Christ Was Anointed into These Three Offices
subpoints: The title 'Christ' (Greek) = 'Messiah' (Hebrew) = 'the Anointed One' — the title itself proves the point
Prophet: Deuteronomy 18 prophesied a prophet like Moses; the people of Jesus' day expected the Messiah to be a prophet, and Jesus never corrected that assumption (Mark 6:14-15; John 6:14)
Jesus applied Isaiah 61:1-3 to Himself in the synagogue at Nazareth (Luke 4:16-22): 'Today this scripture is fulfilled in your hearing' — a prophetic ministry of proclamation
Hebrews 1:1-5: God spoke by the prophets, but has in these last days spoken by His Son — Jesus is the last and greatest prophet
Priest: Hebrews 3:1-2 calls Jesus 'the high priest of our confession'
King: Psalm 45:6-7, applied to Jesus in Hebrews — 'Your throne, O God, is forever and ever'; God the Father has anointed the Son with the oil of gladness
Baptism: Jesus' baptism was His formal inauguration into these offices — the Spirit descended on Him (the anointing), and the Father declared Him 'My beloved Son' (Matthew 3:13-17)

outline.title: How Christ Executes the Office of Prophet
subpoints: A prophet's primary function is not predicting the future but proclaiming God's Word to God's people, calling them back to God
Christ is not merely a prophet among prophets; He is the prophet from whom all other prophets derive their prophetic office
John 1:18: 'The only begotten Son, who is in the bosom of the Father, He has declared Him' — the prophetic office
Christ has always been the one who reveals God, even before the incarnation — the burning bush, the pillar of cloud and fire, the rock giving water were all pre-incarnate appearances of the Son
1 Peter 1:10-11: 'The Spirit of Christ was in them' — the words of Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel are the words of Christ; all of Scripture from Genesis to Revelation are the words of Christ
Christ revealed the whole will of God through the apostles: He gave them all things He heard from the Father (John 15); their writings are the commandments of the Lord (1 Corinthians 14:37)

Two applications: (1) Jesus speaks to us authoritatively and infallibly today through the Bible; (2) If we want to hear from God, we read the Bible and listen to it preached — there is no other direct, authoritative, infallible word from God outside Scripture

summary: This is Part 4 of the Person and Work of Christ Sunday school series, covering Westminster Larger Catechism Questions 42-43. Pastor Lyro reviews the previous lesson's ground on the title 'Christ' (anointed one) and then works through two related questions: Were prophets, priests, and kings in the Old Testament actually initiated into their offices by anointing? And was Jesus Christ Himself anointed into these three offices? After demonstrating from Exodus 28, 1 Samuel 16, Psalm 105, and 1 Kings 19 that the answer to the first question is yes, he turns to Jesus. The title 'Christ' itself proves the point about anointing. Jesus is established as a prophet by Deuteronomy 18's prophecy of a prophet like Moses; by the popular expectation of His day which He never corrected; by His own claim to fulfill Isaiah 61 in Luke 4; and by Hebrews 1, which presents Him as the climactic voice through whom God speaks. He is briefly established as priest (Hebrews 3) and king (Psalm 45 applied to Him in Hebrews). His baptism — with the Spirit descending and the Father's voice declaring Him Son — is identified as His formal inauguration into all three offices. The lesson concludes by exploring how Christ executes the prophetic office: not as one prophet among others but as the eternal revealer of God who spoke through all the prophets by His Spirit, and who now speaks through the completed canon of Scripture. The two applications are that Christ speaks authoritatively and infallibly today, and that He does so through the Bible — which is the only place we can hear from God with certainty.
transcript:
## Review: The Westminster Larger Catechism Q. 42-43

We're on a Sunday school series concerning the person and work of Jesus Christ. We have seen that Jesus Christ is the mediator of the covenant of grace, that He is fully man and fully God in one person with two distinct natures united in one person, and that He executes the offices of prophet, priest, and king. In the Bible, a person would be initiated into these offices generally by way of being anointed, and that is why Jesus was given the title of Christ — the anointed one.

Question 42 of the Westminster Larger Catechism asks: Why was our mediator called Christ? Answer: Because He was anointed with the Holy Ghost above measure, and so set apart and fully furnished with all authority and ability to execute the office of prophet, priest, and king of His church, in the state of both His humiliation and exaltation.

Question 43 asks: How does Christ execute the office of a prophet? Answer: Christ executes the office of a prophet in His revealing to the church in all ages by His spirit and word, and in diverse ways of administration, the whole will of God in all things concerning their edification and salvation.

Before we focus on how Jesus executes the office of prophet, we need to answer two questions: Were prophets, priests, and kings actually anointed as an initiation into their respective offices? And was Christ anointed into these offices as well?

## Prophets, Priests, and Kings Were Anointed

It is easy to demonstrate that priests and kings in Israel were inaugurated into their offices by anointing. In Exodus 28, the nation is instructed how to start Aaron and his sons into the office of priest. The Scriptures say: "You shall anoint them, consecrate them, and sanctify them, that they may minister to Me as priests."

With David being anointed king in 1 Samuel 16: "The Lord said to Samuel, how long will you mourn for Saul, seeing I have rejected him from reigning over Israel? Fill your horn with oil and go; I am sending you to Jesse the Bethlehemite. For I have provided myself a king among his sons." And if you continue reading chapter 16, you see Samuel anoints David to be king over Israel.

It takes a little more work to demonstrate that prophets were also generally initiated into their office by anointing, but it is there. Psalm 105:13-15: "When they went from one nation to another, from one kingdom to another people, He permitted no one to do them wrong; yes, He rebuked kings for their sakes, saying, 'Do not touch my anointed ones, and do my prophets no harm.'" Hebrews is famous for its parallelism — here "my anointed ones" is set in parallel to "my prophets," meaning they refer to the same people. God sees His prophets as the anointed ones.

We see also that Elijah was ordered by God to anoint Elisha to be a prophet: "Also, you shall anoint Jehu the son of Nimshi as king over Israel; and Elisha the son of Shaphat of Abel Meholah you shall anoint as prophet in your place." So prophets too were initiated into their offices by way of anointing.

## Christ Was Anointed into These Offices

Now we need to determine that the Bible wants us to think of Jesus in terms of these three offices. We start by looking at the title itself. The word "Christ" is just the Greek word for the term "Messiah," which is the Hebrew word for "the anointed one." So just the fact that Jesus is called "the Christ" — as Andrew pointed out last week — is not called that because He was born from Mr. Christ and Mrs. Christ. It is a title given to Jesus of being the anointed one.

In addition, the Bible positively teaches that Jesus was anointed into the office of prophet. It was the expectation of God's people that the Messiah would be a prophet, and Jesus never defeated that assumption. This rests on Deuteronomy 18, where God says to Moses: "I will raise up for them a prophet like you from among their brethren, and I will put my words in his mouth, and he shall speak to them all that I command him." The translators of the New King James capitalize "prophet" there as a reference to the Messiah.

In Mark 6:14-15 we read: "Now King Herod heard of Him, for His name had become well known, and he said, 'John the Baptist is risen from the dead, and therefore these powers are at work in him.' Others said, 'It is Elijah.' And others said, 'It is the Prophet, or like one of the prophets.'" Jesus never challenged the idea that He was the prophet, though He did challenge the idea that He was John the Baptist or Elijah.

In John 6:14: "Then those men, when they had seen the sign that Jesus did, said, 'This is truly the Prophet who is to come into the world.'" That was the assumption of God's people at the time of Jesus, and Jesus never challenged it.

But Jesus Himself saw Himself as a prophet — one who brings the word of God to God's people. Turn to Isaiah 61:1-3. Isaiah 61 is in the hopeful section of Isaiah — starting at chapter 40 there is a turn toward the coming Messiah, and Isaiah 61 is one of those passages. Verses 1-3: "The Spirit of the Lord God is upon Me, because the Lord has anointed Me to preach good tidings to the poor; He has sent Me to heal the brokenhearted, to proclaim liberty to the captives and the opening of the prison to those who are bound; to proclaim the acceptable year of the Lord, and the day of vengeance of our God; to comfort all who mourn."

Notice how the ministry described in Isaiah 61 is a ministry of proclamation — that is a prophetic ministry. And Jesus applies these words to Himself. Luke 4:16-22: "So He came to Nazareth, where He had been brought up. And as His custom was, He went into the synagogue on the Sabbath day, and stood up to read. And He was handed the book of the prophet Isaiah. And when He had opened the book, He found the place where it was written" — Isaiah 61:1-2. He reads it, then closes the book, gives it back to the attendant, sits down, and with the eyes of all fixed on Him He begins His sermon: "Today this scripture is fulfilled in your hearing." He was claiming to be the fulfillment of Isaiah 61.

We can also see that Jesus executed the office of priest. Hebrews 3:1-2: "Therefore, holy brethren, partakers of the heavenly calling, consider the Apostle and High Priest of our confession, Christ Jesus, who was faithful to Him who appointed Him." And as king: Psalm 45:6-7 says, "Your throne, O God, is forever and ever; a scepter of righteousness is the scepter of Your kingdom... Therefore God, Your God, has anointed You with the oil of gladness more than Your companions." The author of Hebrews attributes that psalm to Jesus Christ.

And Jesus was formally anointed at His baptism. In Matthew 3:13-17: "Then Jesus came from Galilee to John at the Jordan to be baptized by him... When He had been baptized, Jesus came up immediately from the water... and He saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove and alighting upon Him. And suddenly a voice came from heaven, saying, 'This is My beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased.'" Remember Isaiah 61 — "The Spirit of the Lord has anointed Me" — here is that anointing of the Spirit upon Jesus Christ.

## How Christ Executes the Prophetic Office

Now we are ready to understand how Christ executes the office of prophet. Jesus is the prophet at the top — He is the prophet from whom all other prophets derived their prophetic office. It was not that there was a line of prophets and Jesus was the prophet at the end; Jesus was always being the prophet, and it is from His office as prophet that all the other prophets derived their office.

John 1:18: "No one has seen God at any time. The only begotten Son, who is in the bosom of the Father, He has declared Him." It is the Son who declares who the Father is — that is the prophetic office.

Christ has always been the one who reveals God in all ages through His Spirit. He did not start doing that at His incarnation. Anytime God interacted with people in the Old Testament — the burning bush, the pillar of cloud by day and the pillar of fire by night, the rock that gave them water in the wilderness — these were pre-incarnate appearances of the Lord Jesus Christ.

1 Peter 1:10-11: "Of this salvation the prophets have inquired and searched carefully, who prophesied of the grace that would come to you, searching what, or what manner of time, the Spirit of Christ who was in them was indicating when He testified beforehand the sufferings of Christ and the glories that would follow." So when we read Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and all the other prophets — what we read is what the Spirit of Christ who was in them revealed and led them to write. When you open your Bible, don't think that only the red-letter words are the words of Christ. The words of Isaiah, the words of any prophet, and we could claim the words anywhere in the Bible from Genesis 1 to Revelation 22 — these are the words of Christ.

We saw that in Hebrews 1:1-2: "God, who at various times and in various ways spoke in time past to the fathers by the prophets, has in these last days spoken to us by His Son." So Christ is the climactic voice of God, the last and greatest prophet.

There are at least two massively important applications of the truth that Christ executes the office of prophet. First: Jesus speaks to us authoritatively and infallibly today, through the Bible. Second: Jesus speaks to us through the Bible. If we want to hear from God, we will read the Bible and listen to it preached. There is no other direct word of God outside the Bible that is infallible and authoritative. The apostles themselves expected the church to look to their words if she wanted to know what to believe and what to do.

We may receive subjective promptings that may be the work of the Spirit — such as the Lord bringing a verse to our mind, or helping us know what to say in a difficult moment. Those promptings are real, but they are subjective, and they must always be checked against the objective and infallible truth of the Bible. If a prompting goes against Scripture, or is about something Scripture specifically addresses, that is not a prompting coming from the Holy Spirit.

Father in heaven, thank you for your goodness to us. We think of that we have a Savior who is also a prophet, one who declares and proclaims all things concerning salvation and practice. We thank You that He has always done that and continues to do that through Your Word. Even as we gather together this morning to worship You, we pray that Your Spirit will be present with us, applying the word of Christ as our prophet to us. We pray in Jesus' name. Amen.
